expectations.text: The document is complete to the skill's template: all 15 sections are present and filled, and the Decision Coverage Checklist accounts for every one of the 22 taxonomy dimensions (A1-A14 and B1-B8) with a status (Resolved / Assumed / N/A) and a substantive note — no row left blank or rubber-stamped.
expectations.evidence: Document has exactly 15 numbered sections (§1 Summary through §15 Decision Coverage Checklist) plus Appendix A. §15 contains all 22 rows (A1–A14 and B1–B8), each with a status and a non-trivial note. Example: A14 reads 'Assumed — Blueprint does not specify a11y requirements… Assumed: WCAG 2.1 AA color contrast for the grid, ARIA label on the grid, keyboard-accessible Refresh button.' B7 reads 'Assumed (A5) — GITHUB_WIDGET_ENABLED flag; confirm flag system with team — §10.' No row is blank or rubber-stamped.

expectations.text: Assumptions and Accepted Compromises are each first-class tables (not prose); every accepted compromise names the specific design principle it bends and gives a concrete revisit trigger.
expectations.evidence: §11 is a 4-column Assumptions table (9 rows, A1–A9). §12 is a 6-column Accepted Compromises table (5 rows, C1–C5) with a dedicated 'Principle bent' column and a 'Revisit trigger' column. Every row names a principle explicitly: C1 'Principle 8 (Measure, don't guess)', revisit trigger 'If the product adds email digests'; C2 'Principle 6 (bounded operations)', trigger 'If Redis availability SLO degrades'; C5 'Principle 11 (Security & privacy)', trigger 'If the scope is ever expanded to include write permissions.'

expectations.text: The design guarantees that a visitor viewing a profile never triggers a GitHub API call — reads are always served from stored data — and this is stated as a deliberate design property, not left incidental. (Correctness trap: a naive design fetches on page view.)
expectations.evidence: §1 frames the core architectural choice as 'served directly from the database on every page load — visitors never hit the GitHub API.' D1 §3 states as an explicit decision: 'No read request, from any user, ever triggers a live GitHub API call.' The §8 adversarial table entry 'Visitor triggers a data fetch' lists the mitigation: 'Visitors hit a pure DB-read endpoint. No fetch is ever triggered by an unauthenticated or non-owner request.' This is stated three times as a deliberate property, not incidental.

expectations.text: The once-per-hour owner refresh cooldown is enforced server-side in a way a scripted client cannot bypass (server-checked timestamp, lock, or equivalent), explicitly addressing the blueprint's adversarial 'scripting the request' scenario — not merely a disabled button or client-side timer.
expectations.evidence: D4 specifies a Redis key 'github_refresh_cooldown:<user_id> with a 3600-second TTL… the POST endpoint checks this key before enqueuing; if present, it returns 429.' D4 quotes the blueprint adversarial scenario directly: 'The blueprint's adversarial scenario ("scripting the request") requires a server-side, cross-process lock. Redis is the right answer.' The §8 adversarial table adds: 'Rate-limiting is per user_id (not per IP), so proxying doesn't help the attacker.'

expectations.text: The design specifies what the owner and visitors each see for all three GitHub-failure deviation scenarios — GitHub unreachable/slow, token revoked/expired, and a rate-limit response — with a concrete mechanism for each, and never surfaces a hard error to visitors.
expectations.evidence: §7 covers all three with concrete mechanisms: (1) GitHub unreachable: Celery retry policy (3x exponential backoff), last snapshot always served, 'Last updated <time>' label shown; visitors see no error. (2) Token revoked/expired: task catches 401, sets token_status='revoked'/'expired', owner sees 'Reconnect GitHub', visitors see last stored grid. (3) Rate limit 429: respects Retry-After header via Celery countdown, sets fetch_status='error', owner sees 'Couldn't refresh right now, try again later', visitors see last snapshot. All three visitor experiences are error-free.

expectations.text: The design explicitly names the central architectural trade-off (serving stored, possibly-stale data to avoid GitHub API load) and resolves it with principle-grounded reasoning about the freshness-vs-cost balance.
expectations.evidence: D1 states: 'Trade-off accepted: Data shown to visitors can be up to 24 hours stale. This is explicitly allowed by the blueprint and is the core freshness-vs-cost trade-off the product owner accepted.' The resolution cites Principle 13 (Cache invalidation is a design decision) and Principle 12 (Cost as a first-class constraint). §12 C3 labels it 'Principle 8 (Measure for accuracy)' with the revisit trigger 'If the product changes the freshness requirement.' §1 calls the background-cache model 'the core architectural choice.'

expectations.text: OAuth token handling is specified concretely — encrypted at rest and never returned in any API response or exposed to visitors — addressing the blueprint's scenario that a visitor must never see another member's token.
expectations.evidence: §4 schema defines `access_token_enc BYTEA NOT NULL` with table invariant: 'access_token_enc is never readable in API responses.' §8 Secrets handling: 'Per-user OAuth access tokens are encrypted at rest using a symmetric key (e.g. Fernet / cryptography library)… Tokens are never included in logs, error messages, or API responses.' §8 adversarial table: 'The token is stored encrypted in Postgres and never serialized in any API response. The widget read endpoint returns only contribution data, not connection metadata.'

expectations.text: GitHub API consumption is bounded: background refresh runs only for stale data and cannot fan out without limit, and per-call cost or quota is acknowledged — directly serving the user's stated priority of not hammering the API.
expectations.evidence: D5 adopts lazy read-time refresh 'only [for] profiles that are actually being viewed' plus an in-flight guard (Redis key github_fetch_in_flight:<user_id>) preventing duplicate tasks. §6 quantifies the bound: 'at most ~20k tasks/day… roughly 0.23 tasks/second average, well within Celery + Postgres capacity.' §5 external services table acknowledges quota: '5,000 points/hour per authenticated user (GraphQL cost: ~1 point per contributionsCollection query).'

expectations.text: The document is complete to the skill's template: all 15 sections are present and filled, and the Decision Coverage Checklist accounts for every one of the 22 taxonomy dimensions (A1-A14 and B1-B8) with a status (Resolved / Assumed / N/A) and a substantive note — no row left blank or rubber-stamped.
expectations.evidence: All 15 sections present (§1 Summary through §15 Decision Coverage Checklist). §15 contains a table with all 22 rows: A1–A14 and B1–B8, each with a Status ('Resolved', 'Assumed', 'Assumed (Ax)', or 'Resolved (N/A)') and a substantive note pointing to the relevant section or assumption. Example: A3 'Resolved — §3 D3, §7 — DB unique constraints + partial index handle race; transactions for set-default'; A14 'Assumed — SavedViewsDropdown must follow the app's existing component library conventions…'. No row is blank or rubber-stamped.

expectations.text: Assumptions and Accepted Compromises are each first-class tables (not prose); every accepted compromise names the specific design principle it bends and gives a concrete revisit trigger.
expectations.evidence: §11 Assumptions is a four-column Markdown table (rows A1–A11). §12 Accepted Compromises is a six-column table (columns: #, Compromise, What we give up, Principle bent, Why acceptable now, Revisit trigger). Every compromise names a specific principle: C1 bends 'Principle 12 (Cost as first-class constraint)', C2 bends 'Principle 6 (Bounded operations)', C3 bends 'Principle 14 (Least surprise)', C4 bends 'Principle 4 (Get the data model right)'. Each has a concrete revisit trigger, e.g. C1: 'If DB load from saved-views queries appears in profiling'; C2: 'If any workspace has users with > 200 views per table, add cursor pagination'.

expectations.text: The single-default-per-user-per-table invariant and the unique-view-name-per-user-per-table invariant are enforced at the database level (constraint or partial unique index), not only in application code — a substantive application of 'make illegal states unrepresentable'.
expectations.evidence: §3 D3 and §4 both specify: (1) 'UNIQUE (user_id, table_id, name)' index for name uniqueness, and (2) 'UNIQUE (user_id, table_id) WHERE is_default = true' partial index for the single-default invariant. D3 explicitly grounds this in Principle 9 ('make illegal states unrepresentable') and explicitly rejects application-level-only enforcement as insufficient given the race condition.

expectations.text: The design correctly resolves the blueprint's concurrent-save race (two browser tabs saving the same view name): the chosen mechanism actually closes the race (a DB uniqueness constraint with a caught error), rather than a check-then-insert that still has a time-of-check/time-of-use gap.
expectations.evidence: §3 D3: 'The blueprint's deviation scenario ("two tabs save a view with the same name at once") is neutralized by the unique index: the second insert races and raises an ActiveRecord uniqueness error, which the service layer catches and returns as a 409/422.' §7 failure table: 'DB unique constraint violation (duplicate name race) | Service catches ActiveRecord::RecordNotUnique → 422.' The design relies on the constraint at insert time, not a prior existence check, explicitly closing the TOCTOU gap.

expectations.text: Read-only enforcement for shared views is server-side on every write path (rename, edit, delete) — addressing the adversarial scenario that a recipient must not modify the original through any crafted request — not merely hiding the edit controls in the UI.
expectations.evidence: §8 Authorization table: 'Write (update/delete) | Service checks owner?(current_user, view) before any mutation | 403'. 'Adversarial scenario A (blueprint): A recipient cannot modify or delete the original view via any API request — enforced by the owner-check in the service layer before any write reaches the DB.' The design explicitly calls out server-side enforcement (service layer) independent of UI controls.

expectations.text: The design prevents a user from accessing or enumerating views not shared with them, with a concrete query-scoping mechanism (and does not leak existence through error differences).
expectations.evidence: §8 Authorization table: 'Read own view | SavedViewsService — WHERE user_id = current_user.id scope | 404 (not 403, to avoid enumeration)'; 'Read shared view | SavedViewsService — JOIN to saved_view_shares matching user or workspace | 404 if no share row found'; 'Enumerate views | List endpoint scoped to (current_user, table_id) + joined shares; no cross-user leak | Only owned + explicitly shared views returned'. 'Adversarial scenario B: a direct GET /saved_views/:id for an unshared view returns 404, not the view.' The document explicitly uses 404 (not 403) on all read paths to prevent existence inference.

expectations.text: The design handles the blueprint's data-drift edge cases — a saved view referencing a since-removed column, and a filter on a since-deleted value — specifying graceful behavior rather than an error.
expectations.evidence: Removed column: §3 D1 states 'The blueprint's edge case ("a column referenced by a saved view is later removed") is trivially handled by ignoring unknown keys at read time rather than cascading deletes or orphan rows.' §4 config schema: 'Unknown keys are silently ignored at read time (forward-compatibility for future config extensions).' Deleted filter value: §3 D1 structural design (jsonb stored as-is) means stale filter values are preserved and return zero results — the filter is kept but matches nothing, consistent with the blueprint's specified behavior. The lenient-read policy at §4 ('Unknown keys are silently ignored') and the jsonb approach together handle both drift cases.

expectations.text: The design names the central data-model decision (how a view's filter/sort/column configuration is stored, and how sharing is represented) and justifies it with principle-grounded reasoning that treats the schema as the hard-to-change thing.
expectations.evidence: D1 (§3) names the config-storage decision explicitly ('Store view configuration as a validated JSON column (not normalized rows)') and justifies it with named principles: 'Principle 1 (simplicity first)', 'Principle 7 (two-way door: if a future filter type requires a shape change, only the validation schema updates, not the table structure)', and 'Principle 9 (make illegal states unrepresentable)'. D2 names the sharing-representation decision ('Ownership and sharing via a separate saved_view_shares join table') and justifies it with 'Principle 3 (high cohesion, loose coupling)' and 'Principle 9'. Both explicitly treat schema changes as costly: D1 trade-off states 'If a future requirement needs "find all views using filter X," a migration to add a derived column or a GIN index on the jsonb would be required.'

expectations.text: The document is complete to the skill's template: all 15 sections are present and filled, and the Decision Coverage Checklist accounts for every one of the 22 taxonomy dimensions (A1-A14 and B1-B8) with a status (Resolved / Assumed / N/A) and a substantive note — no row left blank or rubber-stamped.
expectations.evidence: All 15 numbered sections are present (1. Summary through 15. Decision Coverage Checklist). Section 15 contains a table with all 22 dimensions: A1–A14 and B1–B8. Every row has a status (20 Resolved, 1 Assumed, 1 N/A) and a substantive note citing the specific section or decision where each dimension is addressed, e.g., 'A3 Concurrency & consistency | Resolved | §7, D2 — digest_sends unique constraint; scheduler idempotent' and 'B6 Frontend integration | N/A | No frontend changes; unsubscribe redirects to a static confirmation page; feature is email-only'.

expectations.text: Assumptions and Accepted Compromises are each first-class tables (not prose); every accepted compromise names the specific design principle it bends and gives a concrete revisit trigger.
expectations.evidence: Section 11 is a table with columns (#, Assumption, Why safe to assume, Needs confirmation?) containing 10 rows (A1–A10). Section 12 is a table with columns (#, Compromise, What we give up, Principle bent, Why acceptable now, Revisit trigger) containing 5 rows (C1–C5). Every compromise explicitly names the bent principle, e.g., C1: 'Principle 8 (measure, don’t guess)', C2: 'Principle 7 (prefer reversible)', C3: 'Principle 11 (security & privacy by design)'. Every row has a concrete revisit trigger, e.g., C1: 'If per-job p95 query time > 5s, add covering indexes or a weekly activity summary table'.

expectations.text: The idempotency mechanism guarantees one digest per user per week even under a concurrent double-run of the job — it relies on a database-enforced dedup (e.g. a unique key on user + week), not a check-then-send that has a race window. (Correctness trap: an application-level 'already sent?' check is not concurrency-safe.)
expectations.evidence: D2 specifies 'A dedicated digest_sends table with a UNIQUE(user_id, week_start_date) constraint is the canonical idempotency record. Before any work, WeeklyDigestJob attempts an INSERT INTO digest_sends ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING and aborts if the row already exists.' D2 explicitly rejects check-then-insert as 'Race-prone. Rejected: concurrent jobs can both pass the check before either inserts.' The DB constraint is the sole primary gate, not an application-level check.

expectations.text: The design handles the email-provider failure rule correctly: retries are time-bounded, and on continued failure the digest is dropped for the week rather than delayed — and the design explicitly ties this to the blueprint's 'a late digest is worse than a skipped one' rule.
expectations.evidence: D5 states: 'WeeklyDigestJob uses Sidekiq’s built-in retry mechanism with sidekiq_options retry: 3 and an exponential back-off capped at ~5 minutes per retry. After 3 failures, the job goes to Sidekiq’s Dead queue (not retried).' The rationale directly quotes the blueprint: 'The blueprint is explicit: “a digest that arrives late is worse than one skipped.” This rules out unbounded retries or rescheduling to the afternoon.' Section 7 also lists this explicitly: 'SendGrid returns 5xx | Sidekiq retries up to 3 times, ~5-minute window. After 3 failures, job goes to Dead queue; no digest sent that week. | “Dropped for the week rather than delayed”'.

expectations.text: Per-user-timezone send scheduling is designed concretely (how 8am-local across many timezones becomes scheduled sends), and the design handles the blueprint's 'job runs late' deviation without corrupting the seven-day content window.
expectations.evidence: D1 specifies: 'A Sidekiq cron job runs every minute 24/7. Each tick queries for users where EXTRACT(DOW FROM NOW() AT TIME ZONE user_timezone) = 1 (Monday) and the local hour/minute is 08:00. It enqueues one WeeklyDigestJob per matched user.' Section 7 addresses the late-job deviation: 'Scheduler delayed / starts late | week_start_date is computed from the user’s local Monday midnight, not from the scheduler’s run time. Content window is unaffected.'

expectations.text: The design specifies that no email is sent when a user has no relevant activity, and correctly handles the mid-week new-user partial window — both blueprint rules.
expectations.evidence: No-activity guard is handled: Section 7 states 'Activity query returns no rows | Job inserts (user_id, week_start_date, send_status=‘skipped_empty’) into digest_sends and exits without sending.' However, the mid-week new-user partial window is never addressed. D4 specifies the query 'filtering by user_id and created_at BETWEEN week_start AND week_end' with no mention of clamping window_start to user.created_at. No assumption, edge-case note, or code shows the blueprint's rule that a new user gets a digest covering only the days since they joined.

expectations.text: The unsubscribe link is designed to be unguessable and scoped to the issuing account (a signed or random token, not a sequential identifier), addressing the adversarial scenario; and digest content is scoped to only what each user is permitted to see.
expectations.evidence: D3: 'The token is HMAC-SHA256(secret_key, “unsubscribe:#{user_id}:#{week_start_date}”) Base64-URL-encoded.' Section 8.2 states: 'Unsubscribing another user: HMAC includes user_id; a token for user A cannot unsubscribe user B.' Content scoping: Section 8 ‘Content authorization’ states 'The activity query for user U must be scoped to items/workspaces that U has permission to see. The WHERE clause must include the same visibility conditions applied to in-app item listings (e.g., workspace_id IN (SELECT workspace_id FROM memberships WHERE user_id = U.id AND deleted_at IS NULL)).' Declared a 'critical security invariant'.

expectations.text: The design names the central scheduling trade-off and resolves it with principle-grounded reasoning, and the digest-assembly query is bounded — the 'up to ten highlighted items, +N more' cap is reflected as a real query limit, not unbounded fetching.
expectations.evidence: D1 names and resolves the scheduling trade-off with principled reasoning: 'Per-minute cron fan-out instead of a single Monday 00:00 batch job' is the heading, and the rationale cites 'Principle 2 (match existing patterns)' and 'Principle 6 (bounded operations)'. However, the digest-assembly query (D4) never specifies a LIMIT. D4 states the query 'filter[s] by user_id and created_at BETWEEN week_start AND week_end' with no cap. The blueprint’s '10 highlighted items, +N more' is mentioned in the Summary section but is not reflected as a SQL LIMIT or application-level bound in the query design. The query is unbounded as specified in D4.

expectations.text: The document is complete to the skill's template: all 15 sections are present and filled, and the Decision Coverage Checklist accounts for every one of the 22 taxonomy dimensions (A1-A14 and B1-B8) with a status (Resolved / Assumed / N/A) and a substantive note — no row left blank or rubber-stamped.
expectations.evidence: All 15 sections are present (§1 Summary through §15 Decision Coverage Checklist). The checklist in §15 contains all 22 rows: A1–A14 and B1–B8. Each row has a status (most are 'Resolved'; A7 is 'Assumed (A2, A6)'; A13 is 'Assumed (A1)'; B5 is 'N/A') plus a substantive note pointing to the relevant section and rationale. No row is blank or a generic placeholder.

expectations.text: Assumptions and Accepted Compromises are each first-class tables (not prose); every accepted compromise names the specific design principle it bends and gives a concrete revisit trigger.
expectations.evidence: §11 Assumptions is a Markdown table with columns (#, Assumption, Why safe to assume, Needs confirmation?), 10 rows A1–A10. §12 Accepted Compromises is a Markdown table with columns (#, Compromise, What we give up, Principle bent, Why acceptable now, Revisit trigger). Each of the four compromises (C1–C4) names a specific principle: C1 bends 'Principle 1 — Simplicity first / YAGNI'; C2 bends 'Principle 12 — Cost as a first-class constraint'; C3 bends 'Principle 6 — Idempotency & bounded operations'; C4 bends 'Principle 6 — Idempotency & bounded operations'. Every row has a concrete revisit trigger, e.g., C1: 'If product requirement changes to near-real-time (< 5 s) delivery'.

expectations.text: The design stores per-user dismissal server-side so it persists across a user's devices and sessions — it does NOT rely on client-only storage (localStorage or cookies), which would fail the blueprint's cross-device requirement. (Correctness trap: client-side dismissal is the easy wrong answer.)
expectations.evidence: D1 explicitly states: 'A user's dismissal is recorded in announcement_dismissals (columns: announcement_id, user_id, dismissed_at), not in the browser (localStorage, cookie, or session).' The rationale directly cites the cross-device requirement: 'The blueprint requires dismissal to survive across devices and sessions. Client-side storage cannot satisfy this invariant.' Alternatives localStorage and cookie are listed and rejected as failing cross-device.

expectations.text: The design is proportional to a small feature: it does not introduce infrastructure the feature does not need (no message queue, websockets, Redis, or a background job purely to expire banners — expiry is handled at query time), and any such omission is a deliberate, stated choice.
expectations.evidence: §2 states 'No new service tier, background worker, or message bus is required.' D3 explicitly rejects SSE and WebSocket: 'SSE or WebSocket would add infrastructure complexity… for a feature that fires perhaps once a day.' D5 rejects a background job: 'There is no fan-out, no notification dispatch, and no expensive computation. Adding a queue would add a moving part with no benefit.' D6 rejects a scheduled expiry job: 'Filtering in the query is simpler, always correct, and consistent.' C2 explicitly defers Redis with a scaling trigger. Each omission is deliberate and stated.

expectations.text: The 'one active banner per workspace' rule is enforced structurally (e.g. a partial unique index) so that two admins publishing concurrently resolve deterministically rather than leaving two active banners.
expectations.evidence: D2 and §4 define: 'CREATE UNIQUE INDEX uix_workspace_announcements_one_active ON workspace_announcements (workspace_id) WHERE status = \'active\';'. D2 states: 'A race between two admins publishing simultaneously must produce exactly one winner; the DB constraint makes the illegal state unrepresentable without application-level locks.' The concurrent-publish outcome is explicitly described: 'A partial-unique-index constraint violation on concurrent publish is surfaced as a 409 to the losing admin.'

expectations.text: Dismissal is keyed to the banner's identity, not its text, so that an admin editing the banner text does not resurface it for users who already dismissed it — matching the blueprint rule explicitly.
expectations.evidence: The announcement_dismissals table uses announcement_id as the key (not any text field). D1 states the table is 'keyed to user + announcement ID'. The appendix entry 'Dismissal persistence' confirms: 'Storing it server-side also means the poll endpoint can answer "should this user see the banner?" in a single query.' The blueprint's deviation scenario ('A user dismisses the banner, then the admin edits its text: the user still does not see it — dismissal sticks to the banner, not its text') is directly addressed in D1 and Appendix A.

expectations.text: Admin-only authorization for create, edit, and remove is enforced server-side, addressing the adversarial scenario that a non-admin must not publish a banner via a crafted request.
expectations.evidence: §8 states: 'Admin writes (POST, PATCH, DELETE on /api/admin/announcements): protected by the existing admin-role middleware. Any request without a valid session token scoped to workspace_admin or equivalent is rejected with HTTP 403 before the handler runs.' The adversarial scenario is addressed explicitly: 'Non-admin publish via crafted request: blocked by the admin-role middleware check described above — the check happens before any business logic.'

expectations.text: The taxonomy walk is scoped honestly to the feature's real risk: low-relevance dimensions (e.g. external services, heavy scale, caching) are marked N/A or low-risk Assumed with a genuine one-line reason — not padded with invented concerns to look thorough.
expectations.evidence: B5 (External services) is marked 'N/A' with note 'No external services (§5)'. A7 (Cost) is marked 'Assumed (A2, A6)' with note 'No external services; DB queries are cheap; no incremental cloud cost'. A13 (Backward compatibility) is marked 'Assumed (A1)' with note 'Purely additive (new tables, new routes); no existing contracts changed'. The caching row (A6) is marked 'Resolved' but accurately points to §6 where no cache is added and the reason is given concisely. The notes are genuine one-liners that match the feature's actual risk profile rather than padded speculation.

expectations.text: The document is complete to the skill's template: all 15 sections are present and filled, and the Decision Coverage Checklist accounts for every one of the 22 taxonomy dimensions (A1-A14 and B1-B8) with a status (Resolved / Assumed / N/A) and a substantive note — no row left blank or rubber-stamped.
expectations.evidence: All 15 sections are present (§1 Summary through §15 Decision Coverage Checklist). Section 15 contains a table with all 22 rows A1–A14 and B1–B8. Each row carries an explicit status ('Resolved' or 'Assumed') and a non-trivial note, e.g., 'A1 Performance & latency — Resolved — §6 — p95 < 200 ms; budget decomposition provided'; 'A7 Cost — Assumed (A7) — No paid external services; Redis is infra cost absorbed into existing ops budget'. No row is blank or rubber-stamped.

expectations.text: Assumptions and Accepted Compromises are each first-class tables (not prose); every accepted compromise names the specific design principle it bends and gives a concrete revisit trigger.
expectations.evidence: §11 Assumptions is a table with columns (#, Assumption, Why safe to assume, Needs confirmation?) listing A1–A10. §12 Accepted Compromises is a table with columns (#, Compromise, What we give up, Principle bent, Why acceptable now, Revisit trigger). Every row names the principle: e.g., C1 bends 'Design for failure (Principle 5)', C2 bends 'Security & privacy by design (Principle 11)', C3 bends 'Idempotency & bounded operations (Principle 6)'. Every row has a concrete revisit trigger, e.g., C1: 'If UX testing shows users are confused by stale cursors, reduce TTL to 10 s.'

expectations.text: The design selects a real-time transport (WebSocket or SSE) over the existing REST-only backend and justifies the choice specifically against the blueprint's sub-250ms perceived-latency requirement with actual reasoning, not just an assertion.
expectations.evidence: D1 states: 'The blueprint mandates sub-250 ms perceived latency for cursor motion. Polling at any practical interval (e.g., 200 ms) doubles the worst-case latency and multiplies server load with the participant count. Server-Sent Events (SSE) is one-directional and would still require a separate HTTP POST for each outgoing cursor position, adding a round-trip. WebSocket is bidirectional, has near-zero overhead per message.' §6 further provides a budget decomposition: 'Client throttle delay: 0–20 ms; Network RTT (WAN): ~30–80 ms; Server broadcast: <5 ms; React re-render: <5 ms — Total p95 estimate: ~110 ms — well within the 250 ms budget.' This is mechanistic reasoning, not an assertion.

expectations.text: Cursor/presence state is treated as ephemeral — explicitly NOT persisted to the document store (Postgres) — and the design names where transient presence state lives instead. (Correctness trap: persisting cursors to the main DB.)
expectations.evidence: D2 states: 'Presence state is never written to Postgres' and 'Cursor data is ephemeral by definition (blueprint: never saved, no value once the session ends). Writing it to a database would add latency, schema complexity, and meaningless records. In-memory is the correct durability tier for this data.' §4 'No Postgres schema changes. Cursor data is never persisted.' The in-memory structure is named: 'rooms: Map<docId, Map<sessionId, CursorState>>' inside PresenceService, with Redis pub/sub as the optional cross-process fan-out.

expectations.text: The design handles graceful degradation when the presence service is unavailable: the document still opens and is fully editable with no error blocking the editor — matching the blueprint deviation and applying 'design for failure'.
expectations.evidence: §7 failure table row: 'PresenceService unavailable — WS upgrade fails with 5xx — Client catches error; does NOT block document load — Editor opens normally, no cursors shown, no error message to user — Deviation: presence service unavailable — document still opens.' §7 also states the React PresenceClient 'does not surface errors to the user — the editor remains fully functional.' D2 Trade-off section notes 'the blueprint explicitly defines graceful degradation for service unavailability.'

expectations.text: The design defends against the abuse scenario of one client flooding cursor updates — it bounds or throttles the update rate (backpressure) so a flood does not degrade other participants.
expectations.evidence: D3: 'The server enforces an independent per-connection hard cap of 100 messages/second, dropping excess silently for that client.' This is per-connection, so a single flooder is capped without affecting others. §8 Abuse vectors table: 'Message flood — Per-connection rate cap, client-side throttle (D3).' D3 explicitly cites the blueprint adversarial scenario: 'A flood of rapid cursor updates from one client must not degrade the experience for everyone else in the document.'

expectations.text: Cursor identity (name and color) is assigned or validated server-side so a client cannot spoof another user, and cursor data is delivered only for documents the user is authorized to open — both adversarial scenarios.
expectations.evidence: D4: 'displayName and color are always server-assigned from the authenticated session. Client-supplied identity fields are ignored.' Color is assigned by server via hash of userId+docId. D6: 'Before accepting a WebSocket upgrade for docId, the PresenceGateway calls the existing authorization check... to verify the connecting user has read access to the document. If the check fails, the upgrade is rejected with HTTP 403.' §8 abuse vectors table covers both: 'Spoofed identity — Server-assigned name/color (D4)' and 'Unauthorized join — Auth gate on WS upgrade (D6).' Both blueprint adversarial scenarios are directly addressed.

expectations.text: The design handles out-of-order cursor updates (only the latest position per user is shown) and unclean-disconnect detection, and it identifies the central trade-off — adding an always-on transport and a presence tier to a REST-only system — with principle-grounded reasoning.
expectations.evidence: Out-of-order: §7 table: 'Stale/out-of-order cursor message — Sequence number on each message — Receiver discards if seq < last seen seq for that sessionId.' Unclean disconnect: D5 uses protocol-level heartbeat pings every 15 s and evicts after 30 s TTL. Central trade-off: D1 Trade-off accepted: 'The backend now maintains long-lived stateful connections; horizontal scaling requires a pub/sub fan-out layer (Redis). This is the right trade-off: without scale pressure today, the Redis path is additive and does not affect the initial deployment.' D1 explicitly grounds this in 'Design for the measured requirement (Principle 8)' and 'fewest moving parts (Principle 1).' The §1 Summary also foregrounds this: 'The single most important architectural choice is WebSocket over polling.'

expectations.text: The document is complete to the skill's template: all 15 sections are present and filled, and the Decision Coverage Checklist accounts for every one of the 22 taxonomy dimensions (A1-A14 and B1-B8) with a status (Resolved / Assumed / N/A) and a substantive note — no row left blank or rubber-stamped.
expectations.evidence: The document has 16 sections but uses a completely different structure than the skill's template. It is missing: Assumptions (no section), Accepted Compromises (no section), Decision Coverage Checklist (no section), System Placement (replaced by 'Goals and Non-Goals'), Architecture Decisions in ADR format (none), Rollout (entirely absent). None of the 22 taxonomy dimensions A1-A14 or B1-B8 appear anywhere in the document.

expectations.text: Assumptions and Accepted Compromises are each first-class tables (not prose); every accepted compromise names the specific design principle it bends and gives a concrete revisit trigger.
expectations.evidence: The document contains no Assumptions section and no Accepted Compromises section — neither as tables nor as prose. Implicit assumptions (e.g., Celery Beat availability, no Redis for this feature) appear inline in §7 and §6 but are never collected or named. No design principles are cited anywhere in the document, so no principle-named compromise exists.

expectations.text: The design guarantees that a visitor viewing a profile never triggers a GitHub API call — reads are always served from stored data — and this is stated as a deliberate design property, not left incidental. (Correctness trap: a naive design fetches on page view.)
expectations.evidence: §2 Goals states 'Keep GitHub API traffic low: rely on stored data; never fetch on visitor page loads.' §3.2 flow explicitly shows 'No GitHub API call on this path' for the visitor view. §1 Overview: 'every profile visitor sees the cached contribution grid.' The property is stated as a deliberate goal, not incidental, and the visitor flow is explicitly annotated to confirm no API call occurs.

expectations.text: The once-per-hour owner refresh cooldown is enforced server-side in a way a scripted client cannot bypass (server-checked timestamp, lock, or equivalent), explicitly addressing the blueprint's adversarial 'scripting the request' scenario — not merely a disabled button or client-side timer.
expectations.evidence: §8 Rate-Limit Enforcement: 'The 1-hour cooldown is checked against last_manual_refresh_at in the database, not a cookie or client-supplied timestamp. Concurrent requests for the same user are serialised with SELECT FOR UPDATE on the GitHubConnection row to prevent race-condition bypasses.' This is a server-side DB-level lock that a scripted client cannot bypass, and it explicitly rules out client-side approaches.

expectations.text: The design specifies what the owner and visitors each see for all three GitHub-failure deviation scenarios — GitHub unreachable/slow, token revoked/expired, and a rate-limit response — with a concrete mechanism for each, and never surfaces a hard error to visitors.
expectations.evidence: §10 Error States table has separate 'Owner sees' and 'Visitor sees' columns covering all three scenarios: (1) 'GitHub unreachable (last fetch was ok)': owner and visitor both see 'Last grid + Last updated X'. (2) 'Token revoked / expired': owner sees 'Reconnect GitHub prompt', visitor sees 'Last stored grid + Last updated X'. (3) 'Rate limited on manual refresh': owner sees 'Toast: Couldn't refresh right now, try again later', visitor sees 'Unchanged (last grid)'. Visitors never see a hard error.

expectations.text: The design explicitly names the central architectural trade-off (serving stored, possibly-stale data to avoid GitHub API load) and resolves it with principle-grounded reasoning about the freshness-vs-cost balance.
expectations.evidence: The document never explicitly names this as 'the central architectural trade-off' or frames the freshness-vs-cost tension as a trade-off requiring resolution. §1 states 'The design deliberately minimises GitHub API calls by treating data as fresh for 24 hours' — this describes the goal without naming the competing concern (data staleness as a cost of that choice). No design principles are cited anywhere in the document, so no principle-grounded resolution exists. The trade-off is implicit in the design but never surfaced and reasoned through.

expectations.text: OAuth token handling is specified concretely — encrypted at rest and never returned in any API response or exposed to visitors — addressing the blueprint's scenario that a visitor must never see another member's token.
expectations.evidence: §8 Token Storage: 'GitHubConnection.access_token is encrypted at the application layer using Django's django-fernet-fields (or equivalent). The plaintext token is never written to logs or included in API responses. The token is read only inside the Celery worker context; it is not exposed through any API endpoint.' §8 Visitor Isolation: 'The public profile endpoint never returns access_token, token_valid, or any GitHubConnection metadata other than the rendered contribution data.'

expectations.text: GitHub API consumption is bounded: background refresh runs only for stale data and cannot fan out without limit, and per-call cost or quota is acknowledged — directly serving the user's stated priority of not hammering the API.
expectations.evidence: §6 Background Task specifies the Beat sweep selects only rows where 'fetched_at < now() - interval 24 hours AND token_valid = true', bounding refreshes to genuinely stale, valid connections. §12 Rate Limit Posture: 'we call it at most once per 24 hours per user on the background path, and at most once per hour on the manual-refresh path. On a 429 response we respect the Retry-After header.' Quota is acknowledged: 'GraphQL API: 5,000 points/hour per token; this query costs ~1 point.'

expectations.text: The document is complete to the skill's template: all 15 sections are present and filled, and the Decision Coverage Checklist accounts for every one of the 22 taxonomy dimensions (A1-A14 and B1-B8) with a status (Resolved / Assumed / N/A) and a substantive note — no row left blank or rubber-stamped.
expectations.evidence: The document has 13 sections, not 15. Missing entirely: 'External Services', 'Reliability & Failure Handling', 'Observability', 'Rollout & Operability', and 'Decision Coverage Checklist'. There is no section covering taxonomy dimensions A1-A14 or B1-B8. §2 Goals/Non-Goals covers some scope items, §10 Performance and §11 Security cover parts of A1/A8, but there is no Decision Coverage Checklist and at least five required sections are absent.

expectations.text: Assumptions and Accepted Compromises are each first-class tables (not prose); every accepted compromise names the specific design principle it bends and gives a concrete revisit trigger.
expectations.evidence: §13 'Open Questions (resolved as assumptions)' is a two-column table (Question / Assumption Made) with 6 rows — it functions as a partial assumptions table but is not titled 'Assumptions' and is incomplete (many assumptions made in the document body are not captured here). There is no 'Accepted Compromises' section anywhere in the document. No compromise names a design principle it bends, and no revisit trigger is provided for any tradeoff.

expectations.text: The single-default-per-user-per-table invariant and the unique-view-name-per-user-per-table invariant are enforced at the database level (constraint or partial unique index), not only in application code — a substantive application of 'make illegal states unrepresentable'.
expectations.evidence: For name uniqueness: §3.2 specifies 'CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_saved_views_unique_name ON saved_views (user_id, table_identifier, name)' and §9 migration confirms 'add_index :saved_views, [:user_id, :table_identifier, :name], unique: true'. For single-default: §3.4 uses a separate 'user_default_views' table with 'CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_user_default_views_unique ON user_default_views (user_id, table_identifier)' — the unique index on this table enforces at most one default row per user per table at the DB level. Both invariants are enforced by DB constraints, not only application code.

expectations.text: The design correctly resolves the blueprint's concurrent-save race (two browser tabs saving the same view name): the chosen mechanism actually closes the race (a DB uniqueness constraint with a caught error), rather than a check-then-insert that still has a time-of-check/time-of-use gap.
expectations.evidence: §8.1: 'Two tabs submitting the same name simultaneously both hit POST /views. The database unique index on (user_id, table_identifier, name) ensures only one insert succeeds. Rails rescues ActiveRecord::RecordNotUnique and re-raises it as a 422 with error: "name_taken".' The mechanism is constraint-at-insert with a caught error, not a prior existence check. The race is closed.

expectations.text: Read-only enforcement for shared views is server-side on every write path (rename, edit, delete) — addressing the adversarial scenario that a recipient must not modify the original through any crafted request — not merely hiding the edit controls in the UI.
expectations.evidence: §6 Authorization Model: Pundit policy shows 'update?' requires 'record.user_id == current_user.id' and 'destroy?' requires 'record.user_id == current_user.id'. The section explicitly states: 'Recipients of shared views have no write path to the original. The update? and destroy? checks ensure a 403 is returned even if a recipient constructs a raw request to PATCH /api/v1/views/:id.' Server-side enforcement on all write paths is confirmed.

expectations.text: The design prevents a user from accessing or enumerating views not shared with them, with a concrete query-scoping mechanism (and does not leak existence through error differences).
expectations.evidence: §5.1 provides a concrete SQL scoping mechanism (UNION of owned views and joined shared views). §11 states the GET endpoint 'scopes results to the current user — no view belonging to a non-sharing user can leak into the response.' However, the document is silent on what error is returned when a user directly requests GET /views/:id for a view they have no access to. §6 specifies 403 for write paths (update?, destroy?), but no error code or policy is specified for direct read access to an unshared view ID. The document does not address the existence-leakage concern (returning 404 vs 403 on a direct ID lookup), leaving the enumeration gap unaddressed.

expectations.text: The design handles the blueprint's data-drift edge cases — a saved view referencing a since-removed column, and a filter on a since-deleted value — specifying graceful behavior rather than an error.
expectations.evidence: §7.3: 'When applying a view, the frontend filters column_order against the table's current registered columns. Unknown column keys are silently dropped (matching the blueprint's edge-case rule).' And: 'when filters reference values that no longer exist, the filter is applied as-is; a zero-result set is a valid outcome and the user can edit the filter.' Both blueprint edge cases are explicitly addressed with the specified graceful behavior.

expectations.text: The design names the central data-model decision (how a view's filter/sort/column configuration is stored, and how sharing is represented) and justifies it with principle-grounded reasoning that treats the schema as the hard-to-change thing.
expectations.evidence: §3.2 and §3.5 describe the config storage choice (separate JSONB columns for filters and column_order, plus discrete varchar columns for sort_field and sort_direction) and §3.3 describes view_shares, but neither section justifies these choices with principle-grounded reasoning. The only near-justification is §3.5: 'No schema migration is required when new operators are added; JSONB is intentionally schemaless here' — a single sentence with no named principle, no alternatives-considered, and no explicit treatment of the schema as hard-to-change. The sharing table is introduced without any 'why this and not that' reasoning. The document makes the decisions but does not justify them.

expectations.text: The document is complete to the skill's template: all 15 sections are present and filled, and the Decision Coverage Checklist accounts for every one of the 22 taxonomy dimensions (A1-A14 and B1-B8) with a status (Resolved / Assumed / N/A) and a substantive note — no row left blank or rubber-stamped.
expectations.evidence: The document has 13 custom sections but is missing multiple required template sections: no Architecture Decisions section (no ADR format), no Rollout section, no Assumptions section, no Accepted Compromises section, no Open Risks section, and no Decision Coverage Checklist whatsoever. None of the 22 taxonomy dimensions (A1-A14, B1-B8) appear anywhere in the document.

expectations.text: Assumptions and Accepted Compromises are each first-class tables (not prose); every accepted compromise names the specific design principle it bends and gives a concrete revisit trigger.
expectations.evidence: The document has no Assumptions section and no Accepted Compromises section. Assumptions are scattered in prose (e.g., '-- Assumed existing columns' in §2.1, 'assumed existing' in §2.4). No table of compromises with bent principles or revisit triggers exists anywhere in the document.

expectations.text: The idempotency mechanism guarantees one digest per user per week even under a concurrent double-run of the job — it relies on a database-enforced dedup (e.g. a unique key on user + week), not a check-then-send that has a race window. (Correctness trap: an application-level 'already sent?' check is not concurrency-safe.)
expectations.evidence: Section 3.3 'Double-trigger protection' states: 'The UNIQUE (user_id, week_start) constraint on digest_sends combined with INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING ensures that even if the coordinator fires twice, at most one digest_sends row is created per user per week, and therefore at most one DigestBuildAndSendJob is enqueued.' The DB constraint is the primary gate; the worker's secondary status check is belt-and-suspenders. The schema in §2.2 shows 'UNIQUE (user_id, week_start)' explicitly. Section 7 idempotency table confirms 'INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING on digest_sends (user_id, week_start)' as the coordinator-layer mechanism.

expectations.text: The design handles the email-provider failure rule correctly: retries are time-bounded, and on continued failure the digest is dropped for the week rather than delayed — and the design explicitly ties this to the blueprint's 'a late digest is worse than a skipped one' rule.
expectations.evidence: Section 6.1 specifies 'sidekiq_options retry: 3' with explicit retry delays of [5*60, 15*60, 30*60] seconds (total window under 1 hour), plus a hard 4-hour deadline that drops the job even if retries remain. The text states: 'After all retries are exhausted, the Sidekiq sidekiq_retries_exhausted callback marks digest_sends.status = "failed". The email is dropped for the week — consistent with the blueprint rule that a late digest is worse than no digest.'

expectations.text: Per-user-timezone send scheduling is designed concretely (how 8am-local across many timezones becomes scheduled sends), and the design handles the blueprint's 'job runs late' deviation without corrupting the seven-day content window.
expectations.evidence: Section 3.1 describes an hourly coordinator that 'identifies the UTC window [now-60min, now) — i.e., the set of timezone offsets whose 08:00 Monday falls within the past hour' and queries users with matching timezones. Section 4.2 shows the window computation: 'week_start_date = Date.parse(week_start_str)' (the passed Monday date), with window_end derived from that fixed date — not from job run time. This means a late-running job still uses the correct Monday window.

expectations.text: The design specifies that no email is sent when a user has no relevant activity, and correctly handles the mid-week new-user partial window — both blueprint rules.
expectations.evidence: Empty-digest guard: Section 4.4 states 'If all three result sets are empty → digest_sends.status = "skipped", job exits, no email sent.' Mid-week new-user: Section 4.2 explicitly: 'window_start = [window_end - 7.days, user.created_at].max' with the comment 'This handles the new-user edge case: if the user joined mid-week, window_start is clamped to their join date.'

expectations.text: The unsubscribe link is designed to be unguessable and scoped to the issuing account (a signed or random token, not a sequential identifier), addressing the adversarial scenario; and digest content is scoped to only what each user is permitted to see.
expectations.evidence: Section 5.2 and 5.3: token is 'SecureRandom.urlsafe_base64(32)' (256-bit random, not guessable), stored as 'digest_unsubscribe_token' per user (one per account). Section 8.1: 'One token per user: cannot be used to affect another user's account.' Content scoping: Section 8.2 states 'All digest content queries join through workspace_memberships with deleted_at IS NULL and through workspaces with deleted_at IS NULL. A user will never see content from a workspace they have left or that has been deleted.' The SQL queries in §4.3 enforce this with explicit JOIN conditions.

expectations.text: The design names the central scheduling trade-off and resolves it with principle-grounded reasoning, and the digest-assembly query is bounded — the 'up to ten highlighted items, +N more' cap is reflected as a real query limit, not unbounded fetching.
expectations.evidence: The query bounding is correct: §4.3 shows explicit 'LIMIT 11' on each activity query (fetch 11 to detect '+N more'), and §4.1 describes capping highlighted items at 10. However, the scheduling trade-off is not resolved with principle-grounded reasoning. Section 3.1 says 'Rather than one massive job at a single UTC time, the scheduler enqueues per-user jobs at the right UTC moment' but names no design principles, lists no alternatives with principled rejection, and uses no ADR structure. The choice is described but not reasoned through with named principles, failing the 'resolves it with principle-grounded reasoning' requirement.

expectations.text: The document is complete to the skill's template: all 15 sections are present and filled, and the Decision Coverage Checklist accounts for every one of the 22 taxonomy dimensions (A1-A14 and B1-B8) with a status (Resolved / Assumed / N/A) and a substantive note — no row left blank or rubber-stamped.
expectations.evidence: The document has 12 sections with different names and organization. There is no Decision Coverage Checklist anywhere in the document. The following required sections are entirely absent: Architecture Decisions (there is no dedicated ADR section), External Services, Performance/Scale/Caching (§8 has a brief volume estimate under 'Database Considerations' but no dedicated performance section), Reliability, Observability, Rollout, Assumptions, Accepted Compromises, Out of Scope, and Decision Coverage Checklist. The 22 taxonomy dimensions (A1-A14, B1-B8) are never enumerated or assessed.

expectations.text: Assumptions and Accepted Compromises are each first-class tables (not prose); every accepted compromise names the specific design principle it bends and gives a concrete revisit trigger.
expectations.evidence: There is no Assumptions section and no Accepted Compromises section. Assumptions appear only as inline prose, e.g., §5.5: 'Assumption: near-real-time propagation (within the polling interval) is acceptable.' Trade-offs are mentioned briefly in prose, e.g., §6.1: 'A WebSocket push could reduce latency but adds significant complexity for a low-urgency feature.' No named design principles are cited anywhere in the document, and no revisit triggers appear.

expectations.text: The design stores per-user dismissal server-side so it persists across a user's devices and sessions — it does NOT rely on client-only storage (localStorage or cookies), which would fail the blueprint's cross-device requirement. (Correctness trap: client-side dismissal is the easy wrong answer.)
expectations.evidence: The announcement_dismissals table in §3.2 stores dismissals server-side, keyed by (announcement_id, user_id) with a composite primary key. §2 non-functional requirements states: 'Dismissal must be recorded durably (survives server restart, re-login, new device).' The useActiveBanner hook in §6.2 calls the server dismiss API; local state is only a rendering optimization after a confirmed server write. Client-only storage is not used.

expectations.text: The design is proportional to a small feature: it does not introduce infrastructure the feature does not need (no message queue, websockets, Redis, or a background job purely to expire banners — expiry is handled at query time), and any such omission is a deliberate, stated choice.
expectations.evidence: §5.5 explicitly states: 'There is no background job for expiry. Expiry is enforced purely at query time: the GET /active-announcement query filters ends_at IS NULL OR ends_at > NOW().' §6.1 explicitly rejects WebSocket: 'A WebSocket push could reduce latency but adds significant complexity for a low-urgency feature; the simpler polling approach is preferred.' No Redis, message queue, or background expiry worker is introduced. §12 notes push/SSE as future work, not current scope. The omissions are stated as deliberate choices with rationale.

expectations.text: The 'one active banner per workspace' rule is enforced structurally (e.g. a partial unique index) so that two admins publishing concurrently resolve deterministically rather than leaving two active banners.
expectations.evidence: The design uses a boolean is_active column and relies on application-layer enforcement. §3.1 states: 'Only one row per workspace should have is_active = TRUE at any time; this invariant is maintained by the service layer (see §5.2).' The index defined is 'CREATE INDEX idx_announcements_workspace_active ON announcements (workspace_id, is_active, starts_at, ends_at)' — a regular index, not a partial unique index. There is no DB-level constraint preventing two concurrent INSERT/UPDATE operations from producing two rows with is_active = TRUE. The concurrent-publish race described in the blueprint is not deterministically resolved; §7 says 'last write wins' via the transaction, but without a unique constraint a race between two transactions can both succeed and leave two active rows.

expectations.text: Dismissal is keyed to the banner's identity, not its text, so that an admin editing the banner text does not resurface it for users who already dismissed it — matching the blueprint rule explicitly.
expectations.evidence: §3.2 explicitly states: 'Rows are tied to the announcement_id, not to the banner text. Editing the text does not affect existing dismissals, satisfying the "edit does not un-dismiss" rule.' The dismissals table uses announcement_id (UUID primary key) as the foreign key, not any text field. §5.3 confirms: 'PATCH only updates the existing row. No dismissal records are touched.'

expectations.text: Admin-only authorization for create, edit, and remove is enforced server-side, addressing the adversarial scenario that a non-admin must not publish a banner via a crafted request.
expectations.evidence: §5.4 states: 'A shared Express middleware requireWorkspaceAdmin(req, res, next) checks that req.user.workspaceRoles[workspaceId] includes "admin". This middleware is applied to the POST, PATCH, and DELETE admin routes.' §7 security table explicitly addresses the adversarial scenario: 'Non-admin publishes a banner via crafted HTTP request | requireWorkspaceAdmin middleware enforces the check server-side; client role checks are cosmetic only.'

expectations.text: The taxonomy walk is scoped honestly to the feature's real risk: low-relevance dimensions (e.g. external services, heavy scale, caching) are marked N/A or low-risk Assumed with a genuine one-line reason — not padded with invented concerns to look thorough.
expectations.evidence: There is no taxonomy checklist in the document. Without a Decision Coverage Checklist enumerating the 22 dimensions with statuses and notes, there is no taxonomy walk to evaluate for honest scoping. This assertion cannot be satisfied by a document that does not contain the checklist.

expectations.text: The document is complete to the skill's template: all 15 sections are present and filled, and the Decision Coverage Checklist accounts for every one of the 22 taxonomy dimensions (A1-A14 and B1-B8) with a status (Resolved / Assumed / N/A) and a substantive note — no row left blank or rubber-stamped.
expectations.evidence: The document has 12 numbered sections with different names and structures. Missing required sections include: no standalone Assumptions section, no Accepted Compromises section, no Out of Scope section (non-goals are listed inline in §1), no Decision Coverage Checklist section. The 22 taxonomy dimensions (A1–A14, B1–B8) are never enumerated or assessed. Completeness assertion fails on both the section count and the checklist requirement.

expectations.text: Assumptions and Accepted Compromises are each first-class tables (not prose); every accepted compromise names the specific design principle it bends and gives a concrete revisit trigger.
expectations.evidence: There is no dedicated Assumptions section anywhere in the document. §12 'Open Questions / Deferred Decisions' is a table of open questions, not declared assumptions. There is no Accepted Compromises section. Trade-offs are mentioned in passing within prose (e.g., §6.4: 'Worst-case detection is ~45 s; presence:left is emitted at that point, which is within the "a few seconds" spec when interpreted as best effort') but are never collected into a table, no design principles are named, and no revisit triggers are provided.

expectations.text: The design selects a real-time transport (WebSocket or SSE) over the existing REST-only backend and justifies the choice specifically against the blueprint's sub-250ms perceived-latency requirement with actual reasoning, not just an assertion.
expectations.evidence: §2 Decision #1 rationale reads: 'Bi-directional, low-latency, and widely supported. REST-only today means adding one new transport; Socket.IO has proven Node.js integration and handles reconnects automatically.' Decision #3 adds '20 updates/sec is more than enough for the 250 ms target.' These are assertions, not mechanistic reasoning. The document does not explain why WebSocket achieves the 250ms budget while polling or SSE would fail it, does not model latency components, and does not quantify how polling intervals interact with the target. The 250ms reference appears once as a passing claim without supporting analysis.

expectations.text: Cursor/presence state is treated as ephemeral — explicitly NOT persisted to the document store (Postgres) — and the design names where transient presence state lives instead. (Correctness trap: persisting cursors to the main DB.)
expectations.evidence: §2 Decision #2: 'Presence state lives only in-memory (Redis pub/sub across nodes) — never Postgres. Cursor data is explicitly ephemeral.' §4.1 names the storage: 'In-Memory Presence Record (Redis hash, key presence:{socketId})' with a 5-minute TTL. The design actively avoids Postgres for cursor state and names Redis as the transient store.

expectations.text: The design handles graceful degradation when the presence service is unavailable: the document still opens and is fully editable with no error blocking the editor — matching the blueprint deviation and applying 'design for failure'.
expectations.evidence: §7.5: 'If the connection fails or the service emits presence:error, the client silently logs the error and renders no cursor overlay. The document editor remains fully functional. No error banner is shown to the user unless the failure persists beyond a configurable threshold.' §10.3 'Presence Service Failure Mode': 'React client's Socket.IO connection fails; the client catches the error and suppresses it. The editor loads and is fully editable.' Both directly match the blueprint deviation requirement.

expectations.text: The design defends against the abuse scenario of one client flooding cursor updates — it bounds or throttles the update rate (backpressure) so a flood does not degrade other participants.
expectations.evidence: §2 Decision #4: 'Server-side per-client rate limiter (token bucket, 30 msg/sec) — Isolates a flooding client; other participants are unaffected.' §8.2 elaborates: 'Token bucket per socket, Capacity: 60 tokens, Refill: 30 tokens/sec, Each presence:cursor message costs 1 token, On bucket empty: message silently dropped.' The per-socket scope ensures a flooder does not consume another client's capacity.

expectations.text: Cursor identity (name and color) is assigned or validated server-side so a client cannot spoof another user, and cursor data is delivered only for documents the user is authorized to open — both adversarial scenarios.
expectations.evidence: §2 Decision #6: 'Colors assigned server-side from a deterministic palette at join time — Prevents spoofing; client never chooses its own color.' §9: 'Cursor identity spoofing — displayName and color pulled server-side from Postgres at join; client payload for these fields is ignored.' Authorization: '§9: Unauthorized cursor access — JWT verified on handshake; Postgres permission check on presence:join.' §8.1 details the authorization flow including the permission query. Both adversarial scenarios are addressed.

expectations.text: The design handles out-of-order cursor updates (only the latest position per user is shown) and unclean-disconnect detection, and it identifies the central trade-off — adding an always-on transport and a presence tier to a REST-only system — with principle-grounded reasoning.
expectations.evidence: Out-of-order: §7.1 correctly handles it ('apply only if incoming seq > stored seq; discard otherwise'). Unclean disconnect: §6.4 uses Socket.IO heartbeat detection (~45 s worst case). Both are handled. However, the document does not identify the central architectural trade-off of adding an always-on transport and a presence tier to a REST-only system with principle-grounded reasoning. The design table in §2 lists decisions with brief rationales but never names a design principle, never frames the fundamental tension of stateful persistent connections in a stateless REST system, and never articulates the broader architectural cost. The trade-off mentioned in §6.4 is narrowly about disconnect detection latency, not the systemic shift. Fails the 'principle-grounded reasoning' sub-requirement.

notes: v2 assertion set (reasoning-focused). With-skill scored 95.0% (38/40), baseline 55.2% (22/40), delta +0.40. The delta is smaller than v1's +0.67 by design: v2 dropped the harness-dictated filename assertion and consolidated five separate template checks into two, so the baseline is no longer penalized five times over for a single 'no template' failure. +0.40 is the more honest figure.

The v2 suite found a real defect in the skill's OWN output. The with-skill weekly-digest run (eval-2) failed 2 of 8: it never handles the blueprint's mid-week new-user partial window, and it does not bound the digest-assembly query with a real LIMIT (the 'up to ten items' cap appears only in prose). Notably the BASELINE weekly-digest doc DID bound its query (explicit LIMIT 11) - so on that one point the skill output was weaker than baseline. This is exactly the actionable signal a good suite should surface.

What the skill reliably adds: both structural assertions (S1 completeness across 15 sections + 22-dimension checklist; S2 honest assumptions/compromises tables with named principle and revisit trigger) failed in all 5 baseline runs and passed in all 5 with-skill runs.

The skill's value is broader than 'template'. The baseline also failed reasoning assertions: naming the central trade-off with principle-grounded reasoning (eval-0, eval-2, eval-4 baselines all failed it), and structural enforcement of invariants (eval-3 baseline used an app-code boolean instead of a partial unique index, leaving a concurrent-publish race open; eval-1 baseline left the view-enumeration / existence-leak gap unaddressed). With-skill runs passed these. So the skill drives principle-grounded trade-off articulation and 'make illegal states unrepresentable' moves the baseline frequently misses.

Non-discriminating but still useful: several correctness-trap assertions passed in BOTH configurations - visitors-never-fetch (eval-0), ephemeral presence kept out of Postgres (eval-4), graceful degradation (eval-4), flood/backpressure (eval-4), server-side identity (eval-4). A competent model gets these right with or without the skill. They do not separate the configurations, but they are cheap regression guards worth keeping.

Caveat - n=1. One run per configuration: with-skill stddev of 0 reflects n=1, not proven stability. The eval-2 with-skill miss in particular could be run variance; before treating it as a systematic skill gap, eval-2 should be re-run a few times to see if the partial-window omission is consistent.

Timing was not re-measured: the v2 pass was a re-grade of the same 10 documents against tougher assertions, so the executor outputs (and timing.json) are unchanged from the original runs. Scope is also unchanged - these autonomous runs still do not exercise the skill's interview (P3), the P2 'share the map' checkpoint, or the P4 last-call gate; the benchmark measures the document, not the live interview.

FOLLOW-UP (post-benchmark): the eval-2 with-skill miss was investigated. Across 4 with-skill runs of eval-2, the mid-week new-user partial-window assertion failed 3 times and the bounded-query assertion 2 times - a real gap, not noise. Root cause: the skill had a forcing function for architectural-dimension completeness (the Decision Coverage Checklist) but none for blueprint-behavior completeness, so an edge case mapping to no single taxonomy dimension could slip through. Fix: a Blueprint Coverage Checklist (template section 16) that maps every blueprint behavior, edge case, and deviation/adversarial scenario to where the design handles it. Verification: 3 fresh eval-2 runs with the improved skill each scored 8/8 - both previously-failing assertions now pass 3/3. Gap closed. The runs/scores above still reflect the PRE-fix skill; a full 5-eval iteration-2 re-run would be needed to refresh them.
